one_line_takeaway: By encoding the entire poisoning-attack, SGD-training, and test-evaluation pipeline as one mixed-integer quadratic program, this paper obtains the first sound-and-complete (i.e., non-relaxed) certificates against training-time data-poisoning attacks, at the cost of scalability limited to small, linear models on small datasets.
key_finding: On the Iris dataset (unbounded attack, n=8, 4 training epochs, batch size 20, learning rate 0.03), the ablation in Figure 1 shows OBBT substantially tightens the test-data big-M gap |U^(i,test) - L^(i,test)|, with the Auxiliary Variable Formulation (AVF) providing additional tightening on top of OBBT; these tightening strategies accelerate convergence of the dual bound (certificate) but have little effect on finding the primal (attack) solution, whereas adding the local-search primal heuristic substantially reduces the time to prove global optimality. On the Diabetes dataset, an optimal denial-of-service attack with n=50 poisoned samples and label perturbation bound nu=0.5 (epsilon=0) is shown (Fig. 2) to induce oscillatory, non-converging training loss versus the clean baseline. On the Halfmoons dataset, label-flipping attacks and matching certificates are computed across n=0 to 20 flipped labels (Fig. 3); the solver proves global optimality only for n<6, while for n>=6 it still returns a valid but not-necessarily-tight sound certificate (dual bound) due to weaker continuous relaxations at larger n. The paper also reports that, on their (deliberately small) benchmark datasets, existing incomplete certification baselines (AGT/MIBP-CERT-style methods, refs [24,25,33,35]) yield only trivial guarantees -- certified test accuracy of 0% -- because those methods were designed/tuned for larger datasets, whereas the MIQCP's root relaxation is provably at least as tight as those incomplete certificates under the same threat model since it reuses the same relaxation-derived big-M constants.
core_contribution: The paper introduces the first sound AND complete verification framework for data-poisoning robustness of gradient-trained models. It formulates the entire joint process -- adversarial data manipulation, gradient-based SGD training over T iterations, and test-time evaluation -- as a single mixed-integer quadratically constrained program (MIQCP). Solving this MIQCP to global optimality simultaneously yields (a) a provably optimal poisoning attack and (b) an exact (non-conservative) certificate bounding worst-case attack effectiveness, for a fixed model initialization and fixed data ordering. This is explicitly framed as a superset of/improvement over the authors' own prior sound-but-incomplete work (Abstract Gradient Training / AGT, and MIBP-CERT), which relies on interval or convex relaxations at each training iteration and can produce vacuous bounds.
threat_model: White-box adversary with complete knowledge of the training dataset, model architecture, hyperparameters, parameter initialization and (fixed) data ordering at every training iteration, but whose influence is limited strictly to perturbing the training data (Assumptions 1-2, Sec 3.3). Two perturbation classes are formalized: (1) Bounded Perturbations -- the adversary may modify up to n training samples within an L_inf feature-perturbation budget epsilon and a label-perturbation budget nu (clean-label attacks: nu=0; label-flipping attacks: epsilon=0); (2) Arbitrary/Unbounded Substitutions -- the adversary may replace up to n training samples with entirely new points drawn from a bounded attacker domain X^a, which subsumes clean-label and label-flipping as special cases. Adversarial objectives covered: untargeted attacks (degrade overall accuracy, including a denial-of-service objective that maximizes cumulative training loss to block convergence), targeted attacks (force specific outputs on selected inputs), and backdoor attacks (trigger-conditioned misclassification, achieved by additionally allowing manipulation of test-time inputs). The paper does not claim to defend against black-box, model-extraction, or non-training-data threats; robustness guarantees are conditional on a fixed random seed (initialization + batch ordering), stated explicitly as a limiting assumption.
limitations: The paper is explicit that this is a 'proof-of-concept' restricted to small, effectively linear models (linear SVM / linear regression, with a fixed polynomial feature expansion used only to handle the nonlinearly separable Halfmoons dataset) and small datasets (Iris N=80, Diabetes N=320, Halfmoons N=100); exact certification/optimal-attack computation is stated to be intractable at larger scale, as MIP is NP-hard in general. Guarantees hold only for a fixed random seed controlling both parameter initialization and data ordering (Assumption 1) -- they do not average or generalize over seeds. The reported incomplete-baseline comparison (0% certified accuracy) is against methods originally designed for larger-scale settings evaluated out of their intended regime, which the authors themselves flag as a factor. For n>=6 label flips on Halfmoons, optimality is not proven within the 1-hour/8-thread limit, so only a sound (not tight) certificate is obtained. Extensions to multi-class, deeper/nonlinear networks, and alternative optimizers (momentum, Adam, RMSProp) are stated as possible within the same framework but were not empirically demonstrated (only vanilla SGD and single-layer models were evaluated).

datasets_used: Iris (binary subset, d=4, N=80 train, N_test=20)
Diabetes (Efron et al. regression dataset, d=10, N=320 train, N_test=89)
Halfmoons (synthetic 2D binary classification, d=2 raw / 9 features after polynomial expansion, N=100 train, N_test=40)
quantitative_results: Iris (n=8, unbounded attack): OBBT and OBBT+AVF progressively tighten test-data big-M gaps and accelerate dual-bound convergence; adding the primal heuristic (OBBT+AVF+Heur.) substantially reduces time to proven global optimality (Fig. 1)
Diabetes (n=50, epsilon=0, nu=0.5): optimal denial-of-service label attack induces oscillatory, non-converging training-loss curve vs. clean training (Fig. 2)

Halfmoons label-flipping: global optimality proven only for n<6 poisoned labels; for n>=6, solver returns valid but unproven (sound, incomplete-within-timeout) certificates (Fig. 3), within a 1-hour/8-thread Gurobi 13.0 time limit

Existing incomplete certification baselines ([24,25,33,35]) yield 0% certified test accuracy on all evaluated (small) datasets/settings, i.e. trivial/vacuous guarantees, versus this paper's non-trivial exact or sound bounds
baselines_compared: AGT - Abstract Gradient Training (authors' own prior work, refs [33,35]) - sound but incomplete, interval/relaxation-based
MIBP-CERT / BiCert / FullCert (Lorenz, Kwiatkowska, Fritz, refs [24,25]) - optimization-based (MIP) but per-iteration relaxation, incomplete
Standard (untightened) MIQCP formulation used as an internal ablation baseline against OBBT and OBBT+AVF variants
